summary: Richard S. Sutton presents the OaK (Options and Knowledge) architecture, a vision for a general AI agent that learns entirely from runtime experience without built-in domain knowledge. He emphasizes the need for open-ended abstraction, where the agent autonomously generates features, poses reward-respecting sub-problems, and learns high-level options and transition models to plan in a complex, non-stationary world. Richard S. Sutton presents the OaK (Open-ended Knowledge) architecture, a vision for growing superintelligence through runtime experience. He discusses the necessity of open-ended abstraction discovery, the limitations of simple backpropagation in continual learning, and the philosophical boundary between the agent (the mind) and the environment (the body and reward mechanisms).

principles.statement: Agents should make no design-time commitments to any particular world.
principles.rationale: The actual contents of minds and the outside world are arbitrarily complex and endless. Building in domain knowledge limits the agent; instead, we must build in only the meta-methods capable of discovering that complexity at runtime.
principles.source_quote: Instead, we should build in only the meta methods that can find and capture this complexity.

principles.statement: Agents should learn exclusively from unprepared runtime experience.
principles.rationale: The true power of intelligence and reinforcement learning lies in making sense of raw, unlabeled experience in a massive, non-stationary world, rather than relying on special training phases or labeled data.
principles.source_quote: I think this is what the power of intelligence is and particularly the power of reinforcement learning is that we can learn from unprepared runtime experience.

principles.statement: The Reward Hypothesis: All goals and purposes can be formulated as maximizing the expected cumulative sum of a scalar reward signal.
principles.rationale: It provides a clear, sufficient way to specify goals. In a sufficiently complex world, a simple scalar reward can lead to all the attributes of intelligence without needing additional complex goal structures.
principles.source_quote: The reward hypothesis is that that all of what we mean by goals and purposes can be well thought of as the maximization of the expected value of the cumulative sum of a received scalar signal that we call reward.

principles.statement: Agents must autonomously generate and solve their own sub-problems based on feature attainment.
principles.rationale: Possible sub-problems are too numerous and world-dependent to be built in. The only domain-general way to pose them is as "reward-respecting sub-problems of feature attainment" because the agent has no a priori knowledge of objects or states, only the features it has constructed.
principles.source_quote: The problems possible sub problems are too various and they're too world dependent. We have to give the responsibility for forming the subpros for posing the problems uh to the agent

principles.statement: Gradient descent alone is insufficient for continual learning; a random search component is required to maintain plasticity.
principles.rationale: Gradient descent is purely deterministic. To overcome catastrophic forgetting and continuously adapt, the learning algorithm must generate variety, such as by continually reinitializing a small fraction of unused network weights.
principles.source_quote: Gradient descent is deterministic and follow follows the gradient. That's not enough. You need something that will generate variety.

principles.statement: The boundary between the agent and the environment should be drawn around the mind, not the physical body.
principles.rationale: The agent's body (like its hands) is part of the environment that it must learn to control. Furthermore, the reward signal is computed outside the agent's decision-making process (e.g., in the hypothalamus) to prevent the agent from arbitrarily maximizing its own reward.
principles.source_quote: The reward comes from outside the agent but it doesn't come from outside our body. Right? The reward is computed inside our head in fact inside our brain but it's a part of the brain that I don't that is not considered part of the agent

principles.statement: True intelligence requires an open-ended cycle of abstraction discovery driven by runtime experience.
principles.rationale: An agent cannot rely solely on prior design to identify important features. It must continuously evaluate state features, form subproblems from useful ones, solve them to create options, and use those to inform new features, limited only by compute.
principles.source_quote: this is the ambitious idea ambitious new idea of oak uh that is to be open-ended in its possible abstractions limited only by compute

frameworks.name: The OaK (Options and Knowledge) Architecture
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing a general AI agent capable of open-ended abstraction and learning from runtime experience in a complex world.
steps: Learn policies and value functions maximizing reward (classic RL).
Generate new candidate state features from existing features.
Order and rank the generated features based on their utility.
Create reward-respecting sub-problems for each highly ranked feature.
Learn solutions (options/policies) to achieve those sub-problems.
Learn transition models (knowledge) predicting the consequences of executing those options.
Plan at an abstract level using these high-level transition models and value iteration.
Maintain metadata on the utility of all components to recursively inform perception and feature construction.
frameworks.source_quote: the oak architecture involves these uh eight steps that we'll be talking about they're all done in parallel at runtime

frameworks.name: OaK (Open-ended Knowledge) Discovery Cycle
frameworks.when_to_apply: When designing an architecture to grow general intelligence and abstractions from continuous runtime experience.
steps: Start with state features derived from observations.
Construct subproblems based on highly ranked or novel state features.
Solve these subproblems to produce options (skills/actions).
Produce models of the options to improve policies through planning processes.
Feed statistics back to inform the feature generation process about which features are useful, driving the discovery of new features.
frameworks.source_quote: the basic cycle is this cycle we have start with the state features bas from those we uh construct subpros from sub problems we solve them to produce options and the options we and uh can produce models of the options which improve the options and the policies through planning processes.

mental_models.name: Design Time vs. Runtime
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating AI systems by separating what is built into the agent by human engineers (design time) versus what the agent discovers through interaction with the environment (runtime). Sutton advocates minimizing design-time commitments to maximize runtime adaptability.
mental_models.example: Instead of building in a domain-specific concept like 'walking' at design time, the agent discovers 'walking' at runtime as a useful temporal abstraction (an option) to maximize its reward.

mental_models.name: Play as Feature Attainment
mental_models.description: A way to understand curiosity and play in animals and infants. Play is not random behavior; it is the agent posing a sub-problem to attain a specific sensory feature or experience, and learning the policy (option) to reliably reproduce it.
mental_models.example: An orangutan swinging or an orca balancing a float on its back are agents focusing on the sub-problem of reproducing a novel sensory feature (e.g., vestibular sensation) to gain control over their world.

mental_models.name: The Mind-Body Environment Boundary
mental_models.description: Treating the physical body (limbs, sensory organs) and internal biological reward systems (like the hypothalamus) as the 'environment' rather than the 'agent'. The agent is strictly the decision-making mind that must learn to control this immediate environment.
mental_models.example: An infant looking at its hands and figuring out how to control them. The infant doesn't initially know how to control its limbs; it treats the hand as an 'interesting sensation' in the environment that it wants to learn to manipulate.

mental_models.name: Continual Backpropagation
mental_models.description: Viewing neural network training not as a one-time convergence, but as an ongoing process where unused capacity is continuously recycled to maintain plasticity and generate variety.
mental_models.example: Instead of initializing with small random weights only at the beginning of training, a small fraction of units that are not being used very much are continually reinitialized with random weights as the agent goes along.

heuristics: It's easier to get the model right than the values right.
Everything has to be done at runtime.
All your value functions, policies, and state transition models must be approximate.
Keep statistics on features to inform the generation process about which are good and bad.
You need a random component to generate variety; gradient descent alone is not enough.

signature_quotes.text: The actual contents of minds are part of the arbitrary intrinsically complex outside world. Arbitrarily complex uh their complexity is endless. They should not buil be built in.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core philosophy behind the 'Bitter Lesson' and why domain knowledge should be deprecated.

signature_quotes.text: I think this is what the power of intelligence is and particularly the power of reinforcement learning is that we can learn from unprepared runtime experience.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against special training data phases in favor of continuous learning from raw experience.

signature_quotes.text: It's easier to get the model right than the values right.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why agents need to plan using transition models: world dynamics remain relatively stable even as the agent's immediate desires or values change.

signature_quotes.text: Even a single state of the world you can't really keep in your head because the state of the world can includes what's going on in all those other agents minds.
signature_quotes.context: Illustrating why all models and value functions must inherently be approximate.

signature_quotes.text: Gradient descent is deterministic and follow follows the gradient. That's not enough. You need something that will generate variety.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why simple backpropagation fails at continual learning and needs a random search component.

signature_quotes.text: The reward comes from outside the agent but it doesn't come from outside our body.
signature_quotes.context: Clarifying the boundary between the reinforcement learning agent and its environment.

signature_quotes.text: Oak has a vision of how to grow super intelligence from runtime experience.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the goal of the OaK architecture.

signature_quotes.text: I don't think it's a problem with neural networks, the structure of neural networks. I think it's a problem with the weakness of of simple back propagation.
signature_quotes.context: Addressing the issue of catastrophic forgetting in deep learning.

anti_patterns.description: Building domain knowledge into the agent at design time.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The world is arbitrarily complex, non-stationary, and contains other agents. Pre-programmed knowledge cannot anticipate this endless complexity and limits the agent's ability to discover open-ended abstractions at runtime.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on conventional deep learning for continual learning.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Conventional deep learning fails catastrophically when asked to learn continually, suffering from catastrophic forgetting and loss of plasticity (the loss of the ability to keep learning over time).

anti_patterns.description: Using special training data phases instead of runtime experience.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails to capture the true power of intelligence, which requires adapting to unprepared, unlabeled information in a massive, unknown world.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on simple backpropagation and deterministic gradient descent for continual learning.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Gradient descent is deterministic and loses plasticity over time (catastrophic forgetting). It lacks the random search component needed to generate variety and adapt continuously to non-stationary worlds.

anti_patterns.description: Drawing the agent-environment boundary around the physical body.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It obscures the fact that the agent must learn to control its own limbs, and it confuses the source of the reward signal, which must be strictly outside the agent's control so the agent cannot artificially maximize its own reward.
